FT_IC1.evidence: Paper explicitly uses ABCD Study data: 'Nearly 12,000 children ages 9-10 years at baseline...The ABCD Study is a longitudinal study following a cohort of 11,878 9-10-year-olds from 21 research sites within the United States.' Data from NIMH Data Archive DOI 10.15154/1519007.

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple specific smartphone/SM variables measured: social media time (Facebook, Instagram, Snapchat, Twitter, YouTube, etc.), video gaming time, texting, video chatting, screen watching. Adapted Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ) and Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ) are specifically problematic use scales.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple inferential tests reported: t-tests comparing SMAQ/VGAQ between sexes (girls vs boys: t(6301)=8.74, p<.001 for SMAQ; t(6312)=35.58, p<.001 for VGAQ); F-tests for longitudinal changes in screen use over time (p<.001); ANOVAs for race/ethnicity differences; Confirmatory Factor Analysis with χ² and fit indices.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric statistics reported: t-values (t(6301)=8.74), p-values (p<.001), F-statistics (F(1, 59124)=65.43, p<.001), η²=0.008, χ² values, CFI>0.90, RMSEA<0.08. Examples: SMAQ means (girls M=6.35 vs boys M=4.96), VGAQ (boys M=11.17 vs girls M=5.06).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis. Measurement paper that reports associations: describes psychometric development and reports longitudinal changes and between-group differences (sex, race/ethnicity) in screen use and addiction scale scores with inferential statistics.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience, 2022, DOI: 10.1016/j.dcn.2022.101150). No indication of duplication with preprint or prior conference abstract.

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply. Paper reports phone/SM as DV (screen usage amounts), but predictors include both demographic (sex, race/ethnicity) AND individual characteristics (addiction scale endorsement patterns). Also reports cross-sectional between-group analyses, not purely longitudinal prospective prediction. Per E14/E15: demographic and addiction predictors of phone/SM use - mixed direction means FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: Primarily a measurement/psychometric paper that describes scale development and validation (SMAQ, VGAQ) while also reporting associations with demographics and longitudinal trends in use.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: 'A sample from the baseline assessment of the ABCD 2.0.1 release in 2019 was largely recruited across 21 sites in the US.' Population-based sample of 9-10 year old children from ABCD.

FT_IC2.evidence: Specific smartphone/gaming exposure measured: 'Participants completed a self-reported screen time survey, including an item asking children to report the time specifically spent on video gaming.' Groups defined as video gamers (VGs) playing ≥21 hours/week vs non-gamers (NVGs, 0 hours/week).

FT_IC3.evidence: Inferential tests linking gaming to cognitive outcomes: 'Linear mixed models compared task performance measures between NVGs and VGs' with covariates. Results report t-tests for behavioral outcomes and analyses for fMRI BOLD signal. Example: 'adjusted means (SE) times for SSRT were 287.3 (9.8) vs 300.1 (9.6) milliseconds (SMD 0.04 milliseconds; P=.02)'.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric statistics extracted for gaming↔cognitive associations: P-values (P=.02, P<.001, P<.002), means with SEs (287.3 [9.8] vs 300.1 [9.6]), standardized mean differences (SMD 0.04, SMD 0.15), D' scores (2.33 [0.03] vs 2.18 [0.03]).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis: 'In this cross-sectional study, cognitive performance and blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) signal were compared in video gamers (VGs) and non-video gamers (NVGs)...using task-based functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI).' Compares groups on behavioral and neuroimaging outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in JAMA Network Open, October 2022. No evidence of duplication. Note: Article was retracted and replaced April 10, 2023 with corrections, but this is the published version with reported coefficients.

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply. Gaming is the independent variable (exposure), cognitive performance is the dependent variable (outcome). This is a traditional exposure→outcome association, not phone/SM as DV. Per coder prompt: FT-EC7 only applies when phone/SM is DV in all analyses with demographic/contextual predictors.
notes: Cross-sectional comparison of cognitive performance between high and low video gamers using both behavioral (SST, n-back) and fMRI outcomes. Video gaming as exposure variable.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses US ABCD Study data: 'Data were obtained from the US-based, multisite ABCD study (21 research sites from 17 states)...tracking >11,000 children, aged 9-10 years at baseline.' Longitudinal data from ABCD Release 3.0.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time as a measured variable: 'more screen time' is listed as a predictor of youth distress in the abstract and results. Figure descriptions and text mention 'screen time' as one of the pandemic-related psychosocial factors included in the machine learning models predicting psychological distress.

FT_IC3.evidence: Machine learning models (XGBoost, logistic regression) report feature importance via SHAP values, NOT traditional inferential test coefficients. Per E4 rule: 'ML — coefficient reported → MET. SHAP/Gini/permutation only → NOT_MET.' Paper states: 'Figure 2 shows the top 20 features by mean absolute SHAP value' and 'Feature importance of the GBT model.' No traditional stat test (t, F, p-value) specific to screen time association reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: No extractable numeric coefficient for screen time→outcome association. SHAP values are reported as feature importance (e.g., λ values shown in figures) but these are not traditional β, OR, r, p-values, or test statistics for a specific screen-time association. Screen time appears in feature importance rankings but without a discrete, extractable β/OR/p-value.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: 'We fitted machine learning models and provided a comprehensive list of predictors of psychological distress in individuals.' Longitudinal data with prepandemic baseline and pandemic-period outcomes (anxiety, depression, stress, positive affect).

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Journal of Adolescent Health, 2022. DOI: 10.1016/j.jadohealth.2021.11.023. No evidence of duplication or preprint status.

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply. Screen time is used as ONE predictor among many in a machine learning model predicting psychological distress (anxiety, depression, stress, positive affect). Screen time is NOT the dependent variable; it is an independent predictor. The model includes both demographic and individual psychological predictors (e.g., prepandemic internalizing symptoms, sleep problems, family relationships), and reports cross-sectional pandemic outcomes. Per E7: this is a traditional predictor→outcome direction.
notes: Machine learning study (XGBoost) predicting psychological distress outcomes from pandemic period. Screen time is one of ~228 features used as predictor; feature importance shown via SHAP, not traditional regression coefficients.
